coredata.dc:title: A visible-light-driven Z-scheme heterojunction catalysts via carbon nanodots bridges: Photocatalytic performance and mechanisms investigation
coredata.dc:creator: He, Niandan
Yu, Zongxue
Yang, Guangcheng
Li, Xuyang
Xiang, Qingcan
Huang, Qian
Liu, Yucheng
coredata.dc:description: In this experiment, a photocatalyst with a Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed that g-C3N4 nanosheets are connected with NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) via carbon quantum dots (CDs) bridges (similar bridge structures) under solvothermal action, where g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by nitric acid pretreatment and secondary thermal oxidation. The composite has higher photo-reduction efficiency of Cr (Ⅵ) and photodegradation efficiency of MB than that of single component. The rate of photocatalytic reduction of Cr (Ⅵ) by the composite material is 21.8 times of g-C3N4 and 4.1 times of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, respectively, and has better chemical stability and reusability. In the whole system, CDs not only acts as a bridge for electron transfer to induce π-π* transition, but also acts as an electron donor and acceptor due to its excellent charge characteristics. It can provide e− for the reduction of Cr (Ⅵ) and consume h+, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of e−/h+ and improve the photocatalytic activity and efficiency. The mechanism of Z-Scheme charge transfer was further confirmed by the capture experiment of active species. The activity remains stable and efficient after four catalytic cycles, which offers great possibilities for practical production applications.

Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism proposed for g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs composites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this experiment, a photocatalyst with a Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed that g-C3N4 nanosheets are connected with NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) via carbon quantum dots (CDs) bridges (similar bridge structures) under solvothermal action, where g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by nitric acid pretreatment and secondary thermal oxidation. The composite has higher photo-reduction efficiency of Cr (Ⅵ) and photodegradation efficiency of MB than that of single component. The rate of photocatalytic reduction of Cr (Ⅵ) by the composite material is 21.8 times of g-C3N4 and 4.1 times of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, respectively, and has better chemical stability and reusability. In the whole system, CDs not only acts as a bridge for electron transfer to induce π-π* transition, but also acts as an electron donor and acceptor due to its excellent charge characteristics. It can provide e− for the reduction of Cr (Ⅵ) and consume h+, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of e−/h+ and improve the photocatalytic activity and efficiency. The mechanism of Z-Scheme charge transfer was further confirmed by the capture experiment of active species. The activity remains stable and efficient after four catalytic cycles, which offers great possibilities for practical production applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Looking back at the history of human development, we have gone through two far-reaching and significant industrial revolutions. Although our society has made progress, environmental pollution has become more and more serious, affecting every aspect of our daily life. From the initial neglect, to now serious threat to our living environment [1]. Especially in the production and manufacturing process of iron and steel, metallurgy, leather, electroplating, printing and other heavy industries. A large number of pollutants that are harmful to human health are produced every year, the most typical of which are Cr (VI) and high concentration organic dyes [2–5]. These pollutants are generally characterized by toxicity, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, teratogenicity [6], hard degradation and serious environmental pollution [7], which will not only cause serious environmental pollution, but also profoundly affect human health and hinder sustainable development of human beings. When these pollutants enter the water system, they will bring the most direct impact on human production and life. In addition, the massive burning of fossil fuels will also bring environmental pollution and increasingly serious energy shortages [8,9]. In order to effectively control environmental pollution and alleviate the current situation of energy shortage, researchers focus on finding a method and new energy to solve the above two problems at the same time. The collection and transformation of solar energy already has more advocates due to its advantages in chemical synthesis, energy production and environmental governance. Developing solar energy related applications and improving solar energy utilization rates are important means to accord with the current concept of sustainable development of human beings [10].

In recent years, photocatalytic technology, a new water treatment technology derived from solar energy, has become a research focus in remediation of water pollution. The advantages of this technology are mainly economic and efficient removal of a variety of pollutants in water [11]. It depends on finding photocatalytic materials with excellent solar energy absorption and rapid conversion, Whether photocatalytic technology can be applied to practical production. At the same time, the photocatalyst can be used as an effective catalyst to remove pollutants from water [12,13]. Through researchers' explorations, many semiconductor materials, such as TiO2, ZnO, CdS, etc, are caught by researchers due to their special photoelectric conversion performance and electron transfer characteristics. These semiconductor materials can be used as photocatalysts to effectively deal with pollutants in the environmental system [14]. However, with the deepening of research, it is found that although these semiconductor materials have great potential in dye degradation and Cr(VI) reduction, they generally have some unsatisfactory characteristics, such as photo-activation limitation [15], photocorrosion [16] and rapid recombination of photo-generated electrons and hole carriers [17]. The instability of the materials and the rapid recombination of photo-carriers are important factors limiting the photocatalytic treatment of water pollutants. Semiconductor recombination is an effective method to solve the problem of rapid recombination of photo-generated electrons and hole carriers in photocatalysts. Therefore, in order to better meet the demand, we need to develop a new type of photocatalyst with good charge separation efficiency, high activity, efficiency, and good reusability.

The basic structural unit of carbon nitride graphite (g-C3N4) is composed of C and N through SP2 hybridization, which has become a hot research material due to its simple synthesis method, good stability and appropriate band gap [18,19]. However, simple g-C3N4 has defects such as narrow absorption range (absorption edge 460 nm) and photo-generated electron-hole pairs have short lifetimes, which largely limit the practical large-scale application of g-C3N4 in future photocatalytic degradation [20,21]. In order to effectively solve the above problems, researchers reported many effective preparation strategies, such as doping [22], noble metal loading [23], heterostructure [24], etc. Heterostructure, in particular, it can realize the stronger at the interface between heterogeneous structure of the electronic coupling. Not only can it increase the utilization of solar energy, in other words, it can be interpreted as open-source, it can also extend the light transport path of the carrier and effectively inhibit the electrons and holes in the compound, also known as throttling. The synergistic effect of the two can promote the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, this preparation strategy has been widely used in photocatalytic technology. Compared with the redox reaction of a single component on the surface of the material, the kinetic reaction rate of the composite material is faster, and the reaction efficiency of the photocatalytic process is greatly improved [25]. After a large number of literature researched, we summarize a number of examples of g-C3N4 heterostructures in photocatalysis, such as Zhang and Xu et al. [26] Surface-linked TiO2/g-C3N4 2D-2D heterostructures were formed, strong load transfer between layers, and significantly enhanced visible light response. InSe/g-C3N4 van der Waals based on the concept of heterostructure, He and Zhang et al. [27] The thermodynamic reaction of photocatalytic water splitting and hydrogen release was continued with InSe/g-C3N4, indicating that InSe/g-C3N4 is suitable for photocatalysis or combined light and electricity applications. Therefore, choosing appropriate materials to form heterostructures can not only effectively reduce the recombination of photo-electrostatic charges, but also synergistically improve the photocatalytic activity of single components. This can make it have good application potential in photocatalytic technology. In this paper, g-C3N4 nanosheets with good dispersibility were prepared by morphology control.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are organic-inorganic hybrid materials formed by organic ligands and metal ions, and have porous properties [28,29]. They are easy to synthesize and have excellent electronic structure characteristics, ideal topological structure, high specific surface area, flexible structure (pore gap) and adjustable function [30,31]. It has attracted extensive attention in CO2 conversion, catalytic degradation, sensing and other fields [32]. In addition, some studies have shown that some MOFs exhibit semiconductor like properties under illumination, so they have the potential to be used as photocatalysts. At the same time, MOFs have a unique metal substituent resonant structure and can form a metal charge transfer pathway specific for the light irradiated linker [33,34], which has also attracted extensive attention of photocatalytic technology researchers. Mahata et al. [35] reported the ability of different zinc, nickel and cobalt based MOFs to degrade organic dyes under UV light. Sedigheh Abedi et al. [36] the addition of amorphous titanium to MOFs has been shown to increase photocatalytic oxidation of benzyl alcohol under visible light. Wang et al. [37] reported the hydroxylation of benzene to phenol using MIL-101(Fe) and MIL-68(Fe) to achieve the purpose of highly selective treatment of benzene. Among many reported MOFs, MOFs with Fe as the metal center show great application potential due to their special repetitive units of ferrite clusters, which creates a strong Fenton reaction under light and stimulates the effective photocatalytic reaction of hydrogen peroxide in water purification. For instance, Shao et al. [38] supported CDs on NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) under solvothermal action, prepared a highly efficient composite photocatalyst with excellent photoelectron transfer efficiency and low charge recombination rate, which was used for the degradation of heavy metal ions and dyes, with significantly improved photocatalytic activity. Xiyi Li et al. [39] found that the new g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe) complex as Fenton photocatalyst showed potent degradation activity against MB.

Herein, a Z-scheme heterojunction composite with layered g-C3N4 loaded with Fe based MOFs and CDs as a charge transfer “bridge” was reported for photocatalytic reduction Cr (VI) and degradation of MB. CDs can act as a local electron acceptor to effectively promote the transfer and separation of photo-carriers and have synergistic effect with g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe). The introduction of CDs forms a “bridge” between the interface of two monomer materials, which can prolong the transfer path of photoinduced charge and further improve the photocatalytic efficiency of the material. g-C3N4 with good dispersion was prepared by acid pretreatment and secondary thermal oxidation method. Then, the thin layer of g-C3N4 was obtained by effective stratification with isopropanol. A certain amount of g-C3N4 was added to the NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) solution to form an effective heterojunction structure under the solvothermal effect, which effectively improve the transfer efficiency of photo-generated carriers and meet the demand for throttling. The reduction of Cr (VI) and the degradation of MB under illumination were systematically investigated in this paper. The structure, indicated chemical composition and photochemical properties of the material were also characterized. At the same time, the effects of active species trapping agent, pH of reaction solution, H2O2 and various operating factors on the photo-reduction of Cr (VI) Fenton photocatalyst were also studied. The stability and reusability of the composite were demonstrated by cyclic experiments. Finally, based on the discussion and analysis of the experiment, a possible mechanism of photo stimulated H2O2 induced Fenton reaction for Cr (VI) photo-reduction and MB photodegradation was proposed.

2-aminoterephthalic acid(NH2–H2BDC, ≥98%) was purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), FeCl3·6H2O(≥99%), melamine(≥99%), potassium dichromate(K2Cr2O7, ≥99%), 1,5-diphenylcarbazide(DPC, ≥98%), anhydrous glucose, NaOH(granule, ≥99%), methylene blue(C16H18ClN3S·3H2O, MB, ≥98.5%), K2S2O8(≥99%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid(EDTA-2Na, ≥99.5%), P-benzoquinone(BQ,98%), N,N-dimethyl-formamide (DMF, ≥99%), methanol (MeOH, ≥99%), tert-butyl alcohol(t-BuOH,98%), isopropanol(≥99%), anhydrous ethanol(75%), nitric acid(65%) and dilute sulfuric acid(35%) were purchased from KeLong Chemical Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China).

g-C3N4 nanosheets with good dispersion were synthesized by nitric acid pretreatment and secondary thermal oxidation method [40]. The obtained nanosheets were dispersed in isopropanol solution to obtain thin g-C3N4 nanosheets [41]. The synthesis path and structure diagram are shown in Fig. 1 . The more details were in the Supplementary material (Scheme 1).

NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs was synthesized by solvothermal method, which was to add a certain amount of glucose during the synthesis of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) [38,42]. Dissolved FeCl3·6H2O and NH2–H2BDC in DMF solution respectively, mixed and sonicated, then the mixture was added to NaOH and reacted at 170 °C for 24 h. The mixture was washed by centrifugation with DMF and MeOH and finally dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs. The more details were mentioned in the Supplementary information (Scheme 2).

In the process of synthesizing NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, and add some g-C3N4 to obtain g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs(Fig. 2 ). The corresponding g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs composites were designated as LP-x, x = 1, 2, 3, and the addition of g-C3N4 was 5, 10, 20 wt% (20, 40, 80 mg).

The phase identification and crystal structure were detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The surface chemical structure and functional groups of the samples were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, USA, WQF-520). The surface morphology and microstructure of the composites were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7500 F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement was operated on a ASAP2460 for the specific surface area and pore size of composite LP-2, which assist to explore the material adsorption process. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, UK) was used to determine the surface composition of the composite and the chemical environment of various elements. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS) was collected on PerkinElmer lambda 850 UV–Vis NIR spectrophotometer. The apparent band gap energy of the sample is derived by plotting (KM × hυ)2 with hυ, where KM is the Kubelka-Munk function. The photoluminescence spectra of the materials excited by 375 nm laser were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The concentrations of organic pollutants Cr (VI) and MB after photocatalytic reaction were determined by ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shanghai Precision Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd.).

The photocatalytic properties of g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs and LP-x composites were studied under normal temperature and pressure by reduction of Cr (VI) and degradation of MB with a 300 W Xenon lamp with 420 nm UV cut-off filter. Initial concentration of 50 ppm for both Cr (VI) and MB. Dilute sulfuric acid solution (35%) and NaOH solution (2 M) were used to adjust the pH of the photocatalytic system. In the photocatalytic experiment, 0.050 g photocatalyst was added into 100 mL Cr (VI) (50 ppm) and MB aqueous solution (50 ppm) respectively. After ultrasonic dispersion, the beaker is put on a thermostatic magnetic stirrer. The suspension was stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 30 min under dark conditions to achieve adsorption equilibrium. In the process of photo reaction, 5 mL sample was taken periodically (15 min), and the solid catalyst was separated by centrifugation. In the presence of acid medium, Cr (VI) will react with 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC, 2 g/L) to form a purple complex. The absorption peaks of Cr (VI) and MB are at 540 nm and 664 nm respectively. Therefore, The photocatalytic efficiency can be measured by UV–vis spectrophotometry for Cr (VI) and MB concentrations40,41.
The preparation methods of Cr (VI) solution, MB solution and colour developing agent DPC solution are detailed in the Supplementary materials (Scheme 3).

Since the composite material has good adsorption effect on Cr (VI) and MB, the dark field treatment of LP-2 was carried out under the same conditions to further determine the equilibrium of adsorption and desorption. After 30 min, the concentration of Cr (VI) and MB tended to be roughly stable, and the adsorption curve was shown in S1.

where C0 is the initial concentration of Cr (VI) and MB; Ct is the concentration at the sampling point when the catalytic reaction has reached t min; k is the first order kinetic rate constant; t is the reaction time.

K2S2O8 (1 mM), EDTA-2Na (4 mM), BQ (4 mM) and t-BuOH (4 mM) were introduced into the photocatalytic experiment to capture e−, h+, ·O2 − and ·OH respectively. In this way, the mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction is discussed. Through the comparison of the experimental results, the main active species affecting LP-x composites were determined.

The XRD spectra of the prepared pure g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs and LP-x composites are shown in Fig. 3 (a). There are two characteristic diffraction peaks in g-C3N4, one is a strong peak at 13.2° and the other is a wide peak at 27.3°, which correspond to (100) and (002) diffraction planes of g-C3N4, respectively [43]. The former is due to interlaminar deposition of crystal planes of conjugated aromatic complexes, and the latter is due to the in-plane repeating tris-s-triazine unit in the crystal plane of g-C3N4. This same as the standard card of Cambridge crystal library and previous reports, which proves the successful composition of g-C3N4. Secondly, the characteristic diffraction peak of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs mainly appears in 9.2°, 10.3°, 13.1°, 16.5°, 18.2°, 18.9°, 20.6°, 26.3° and 29.5°, They correspond to (002), (101), (102), (103), (200), (201), (202), (204) and (302) diffraction planes of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, respectively [38,42]. This same as our previous studies and the XRD patterns and data of standard cards, indicating that we have successfully synthesized the pure product of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs. The lattice spacing of (002) crystal plane belonging to NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs can be calculated as 0.96126 nm by Bragg formula. In contrast, compound LP-x shows an XRD pattern similar to that of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, which proves that the crystal structure of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs is well preserved during the solvothermal synthesis process, and that no other impurities will be introduced after the introduction of g-C3N4. At the same time, compared with pure NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, a new diffraction peak is observed at 27.3°, which becomes more and more obvious with the increase of g-C3N4 content. This is explained by the g-C3N4 characteristic interlayer stacking peak, which indicates the successful introduction of g-C3N4.

The surface area and pore size distribution of composite LP-2 were studied by nitrogen physisorption. As can be seen in Fig. 3(b), based on IUPAC classification, LP-2 exhibited typical type Ⅳ isotherms, indicating that it had obvious mesoporous characteristics27. LP - 2 has a 29.8628 m2/g of specific surface area, Single point desorption total pore volume of pores is 0.091533 cm³/g, mesoporous average pore size of 165.7 nm. The pore size distribution of the composite is not uniform, the large pore size is conducive to more pollutants into the active site, and then adsorption and reaction. NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs is easy to agglomerate and hide each other, but the introduction of g-C3N4 will make the materials more dispersed and cross-link to form a large number of pores, which may be one of the reasons for the large average pore size of the mesoporous, which can also be clearly observed from the following SEM33. Compared with the pure material, the specific surface area of composite LP-2 has been improved to a certain extent, and the addition of CDs will also increase the specific surface area of the material39. The large surface area can provide more abundant active sites, which will facilitate the adsorption of dyes and heavy metal ions, and then promote the subsequent photocatalytic degradation.

For further exploring the molecular functional groups and molecular structures of g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs and LP-x composites. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of each material. In Fig. 4(a), there are two typical characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 at about 810 cm−1 and 3172 cm−1, which are explained by the bending vibration of triazine ring and the N–H stretching vibration of incomplete polycondensation of melamine precursor, respectively [43]. In NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs, the peaks of 3342 cm−1 and 3162 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of –NH caused by the organic linker [44]. At 729 cm−1, the C–H bending vibration of the organic linker can be clearly observed [45]. At the same time, Fe(III) and organic linkers formed distinct and unique O–Fe–O vibrations at 519 cm−1 and 502 cm−1 [46]. Fig. 4(b) is an enlarged red dotted box in Fig. 4(a). For g-C3N4, a series of peaks at 1200 cm−1–1800 cm−1 belong to typical C–N stretching vibration of aromatic group, corresponding to triazine ring (810 cm−1), CN (1637 cm−1) and C–N (1253 cm−1, 1331 cm−1, 1410 cm−1, 1569 cm−1) [40]. In the enlarged view of NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs, the two peaks at 1548 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 are due to the typical carboxyl vibration peaks [47], and the peak at 1255 cm−1 is caused by the C–N stretching vibration in the benzene ring [48]. At the same time, it can be observed that with the increase of g-C3N4 content, the peak value of composite LP-x at 810 cm−1 becomes more and more obvious. As the content of g-C3N4 increases in the composite, the peak value of NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs at 3162 cm−1 gradually decreases, which may be caused by the overlapping of the broad peak at 3172 cm−1 of g-C3N4. Generally, the FT-IR spectrum of the composite LP-x is basically the same as that of the parent material, indicating that the successful composite of g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs does not change the basic properties of the material.

As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the microstructure and morphology of the matrix and composite were observed by SEM. Fig. 5(a) shows the micrograph of the parent NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs. From the micrograph, we can see that the monomer material is a spindle-like structure, which is consistent with the previously reported literature [49]. When NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs is synthesized, if too much glucose is added, a large number of fine CDs will gather together, which may affect the photocatalytic performance. Fig. 5(b) and (c) show the secondary oxidation isopropanol dispersed g-C3N4 and the reaction of g-C3N4 under the same solvothermal conditions, respectively. It can be observed that under the solvothermal effect, g-C3N4 is further layered, and the morphology is closer to the nanolayer. Fig. 5(d)∼(f) are LP-x composites respectively. It can be observed from the figure that g-C3N4 forms interlaced nanolayered structure under solvothermal effect, and NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs is loaded on the layered structure, forming heterojunction structure. Meanwhile, as the content of g-C3N4 increases, the crystal structure of NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs also became more irregular. This may be because the addition of g-C3N4 affected the crystallization of NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe), which can be confirmed by the change of peak intensity of XRD and FT-IR spectra. In addition, due to the addition of NaOH to adjust the pH of the solution and control the purity of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, Fe(OH)3 and Fe(OH)2 will be formed in the presence of FeCl3·6H2O precursor, and part of them will be oxidized to form iron oxide under solvothermal effect, which is conducive to photocatalysis, The other part will adhere to and wrap NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs, resulting in the agglomeration of NH2-MIL-88 B (Fe)/CDs. This can also be observed from Fig. 5(d)∼(f).

As shown in Fig. 6 , in order to further analyze and verify the composite structure of the heterojunction, TEM and HRTEM were used to characterize it. From Fig. 6(a), we can see that the bulk g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) are closely combined. Fig. 6(b) is a partial enlarged view (HRTEM) of the red circle in Fig. 6(a), from which the bonding form of the composite can be more clearly observed. HRTEM images show that the lamination spacing of composite LP-2 is about 0.96 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of NH2-Mil-88 B (Fe)/CDs, which is almost the same as the D value obtained from XRD. NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) is loaded on the g-C3N4, and CDs are generated on the interface of g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe). After introducing g-C3N4, the MOF retains the complete and regular crystal structure, and CDs are generated in situ on the interface of the parent material, which improves the photocatalytic performance of the composite by facilitating the rapid transfer of photo-generated carriers.

The chemical environmental information of g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs and LP-2 was analyzed by XPS (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7(a) shows that LP-2 contains C, N, O and Fe, and the introduction of g-C3N4 makes the intensity of C and N in LP-2 slightly higher than that of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs. The XPS spectra of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs are basically consistent with that of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) because no new elements will be introduced into the preparation of CDs using anhydrous glucose as precursor. As can be seen from Fig. 7(b), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s can be deconvoluted into six peaks35,36,49. In particular, the peaks at 284.21 eV and 284.53 eV can be attributed to C–C and C–H of the phenyl cycloalkyl carbon. The peaks at 285.76eV and 287.97eV belong to the C–N–C and C-(N)3 groups of g-C3N4 respectively. 285.16 eV and 289.09 eV can be attributed to the benzoic acid ring and carboxylic acid group (O–CO) of the NH2–H2BDC organic linker in NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) respectively35,36. The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 7(c)) can be divided into three categories: (1) The peak at 397.86 eV is the Fe–N bond formed by the combination of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) and g-C3N4 without condensation to the terminal NH2 50, (2) the peaks at 398.79 eV and 400.01 eV can be attributed to C–N–C and N-(C)3 51, (3) the N–H bond of the organic linker at 401.39 eV52. The XPS spectrum of Fe 2p is shown in Fig. 7(d). In NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, the peaks at 710.97 eV and 724.39 eV can be attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. 714.46 eV is a satellite peak of Fe41. It should be noted that the binding energy of composite LP-2 has a slight shift of nearly 0.3 eV compared with NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, which may be due to the adjacent N atoms in g-C3N4 providing. The electrons in NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs were transferred to Fe3+, this led to an enhancement of the electron cloud density on Fe3+, which was consistent with the appearance of Fe–N bonds in Fig. 7(c). In general, the successful binding of g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs was further demonstrated using XPS, which was consistent with the TEM results.

The VB-XPS of the parent material is shown in Fig. 8 . The valence band of the parent material is determined with the use of linear extrapolation. It can calculate the band gap and conduction band position of the material by combining with the UV diffuse reflectance spectrum. After reasonable linear extrapolation, the valence band energies (EVB) of g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs are 1.44 eV and 0.63 eV, respectively.

We used UV–Vis DRS to evaluate the optical properties of the materials. The diffuse reflectance spectra of various materials are shown in Fig. 9 . All the composites show similar hybrid spectra, which can be deconvoluted into three bands. The first band with 200–360 nm wavelength is attributed to CDs, the second band is due to 450 nm threshold of g-C3N4, and the last band is attributed to 800 nm threshold of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe). Simultaneously, with the increase of g-C3N4 proportion in the composite, the absorption band strength of LP-x at 450 nm decreases, which is due to the stronger absorption of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs. Calculation of band gap energy in the Supplementary materials (Scheme 4) [50].

The curve of the transformed Kubelka Munk formula is shown in Fig. 9(b). After linear extrapolation, the apparent band gap energies of g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs and LP-2 are 2.71 eV, 2.28 eV and 1.87 eV, respectively, indicating that g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs are successfully hybridized and heterojunction is formed between them. In addition, the composite shows lower apparent band gap energy, suggesting that the material may be a potential visible light (solar) catalytic material.

It is well known that the premise of photocatalytic reactions is the illumination excitation and effective separation of photo-generated carriers (e−/h+). We used photoluminescence spectroscopy to analyze the separation efficiency of the photoexcited charge of the sample, because the photoluminescence (PL) was derived from the recombination of photoelectric charge. Generally, the lower the photoluminescence intensity indicates that the lower the charge recombination rate is, the higher the photocatalytic efficiency of the materials. Therefore, it can be observed in the PL spectrum in Fig. 9(c) that compared with parent g-C3N4 and MOF, the PL intensity of the composites has a great quenching, indicating that the heterostructures formed between parent materials and the “bridge” formed by CDs can effectively increase the electron transport path, Thereby reducing the recombination of photo-generated carriers.

Furthermore, when the proportion of g-C3N4 in the composite were overloaded, the PL intensity increased to a certain extent, which may be the influence of excessive g-C3N4 on the formation and crystallization of the composites. Noteworthy, the PL intensity of composite LP-2 is the lowest, and we can infer that its photocatalytic activity is the best.

As shown in Fig. 10 , we explored the photocatalytic activities of pure g-C3N4, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs and LP-x by reducing Cr (Ⅵ) and degrading MB under visible light conditions. According to a series of previous experimental characterization and result analysis, we speculated that the combination of g-C3N4 and Fe-MOF could obtain excellent photocatalytic activity that a single component did not have. The results in Fig. 10 well verified our prediction. Fig. 10(a) shows the ability of each monomer material and composites to reduce Cr (Ⅵ) at pH = 2 under visible light conditions. The reaction rates (Fig. 10(b)) are shown in descending order of g-C3N4 < NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs < LP-3 < LP-1 <LP-2. After 30 min of dark treatment, LP-2 almost reached the optimum adsorption rate. After visible light irradiation for 90 min, the photo-reduction rate of Cr (Ⅵ) by the composites was more than 90%, while the reduction rate of pure g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs was only 28% and 72%, respectively. Among all the composites, the reduction rate of Cr (Ⅵ) reached 99% after LP-2 irradiation for 45 min, and the reaction rates (k = 0.0454 min−1) were 21.8 and 4 times higher than those of pure g-C3N4 (k = 0.00208 min−1) and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs (k = 0.01133 min−1), respectively. Dynamic fitting histogram is shown in S2 of Supplementary information.

In order to discuss the photocatalytic effect of the composite materials more comprehensively. As shown in Fig. 10(c), after dark treatment for 30 min, pure g-C3N4 and Fe-MOF can only adsorb 7% and 43% of MB, respectively. And the optimal adsorption rate of LP-2 can reach about 72%, which may be because of the introduction of lamellar g-C3N4 increases the specific surface area of the material. And the large pore size can adsorb a large number of ions into the active site, increasing the adsorption rate, so as to facilitate the catalytic reaction. After 120 min of visible light irradiation, 62% of MB was degraded in the presence of g-C3N4, and the MB degradation efficiencies of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs, LP-1, LP-2 and LP-3 were 93%, 97%, 98% and 99%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10(d) that the degradation rates of MB by LP-2(k = 0.02551 min−1) are 3.4 times that of pure g-C3N4(k = 0.00739 min−1) and 1.6 times that of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs (k = 0.01595 min−1), respectively. We found that adding g-C3N4 helps to improve the photocatalytic activity, while an excess of g-C3N4 is not conducive to the photocatalytic activity. It may be due to excess g-C3N4 agglomeration or covering some active sites. From Fig. 10(a)(c), it can be clearly observed that the photocatalytic effect of LP-2 is obvious with the passage of reaction time. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) and MB over time are shown in S3.

In general, when g-C3N4 and MOF are combined effectively, the synergistic effect between the components can effectively promote the photocatalytic activity of a single component. However, when the optimal amount is reached, too much g-C3N4 will weaken the catalytic effect. Part of the reason for this phenomenon is that excessive g-C3N4 can affect the crystallization of MOF, which can be confirmed by SEM and XRD results. At the same time, lamellar g-C3N4 will block part of the incident light and bring more serious agglomeration phenomenon, resulting in the reduction of the visible light receiving efficiency and ability of the composite.

As we know, under alkaline environment, Cr(III) in solution mainly exists in the form of Cr(OH)3 colloid, which may coat the surface of the photocatalyst, thereby reducing its photocatalytic activity. That is why we studied the photocatalytic reduction effect of Cr (VI) under the condition that the reaction solution was acidic. As shown in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), with the decrease of pH value, the photocatalytic reduction efficiency of LP-2 for Cr (VI) was significantly improved. The reason for this trend is that photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) is a catalytic behaviour of acid. It can promote catalytic reaction under acidic condition [51]. Specifically, the surface of the photocatalyst is positively charged owing to a large number of H+ ions were adsorbed from the reaction solution [52]. Under the electrostatic effect, Cr(VI) can be more easily attached to the catalyst surface, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic activity. The results show that the composite material has a good reduction effect on Cr (Ⅵ) under extreme acid condition, and it may play a non-negligible role in practical industrial wastewater under extreme acid environment.

It is well known that photocatalysts produce a variety of active substances such as electrons (e−), holes (h+), superoxide radicals (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) under light conditions. Among them, e− is the key substance for photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI). h+ and ·OH are the foremost active substances for photodegradation of organic pollutants such as MB. With the purpose of investigating the possible reaction mechanism of each composite material, we carried out the capture experiment of the active species during catalysis. It should be noted that e−, h+, ·O2 − and ·OH were captured using K2S2O8, EDTA-2Na, BQ and t-BuOH, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 13 (a), EDTA-2Na and BQ can significantly improve the photo-reduction ability of LP-2 to Cr(VI). The main reasons are as follows: firstly, EDTA-2Na can directly consume h+, and the consumption of h+ is favourable for the photoinduced separation of photo-carriers. Secondly, in the process of photocatalytic reduction, when the solution environment is acidic, the following processes exist:
(3) O2 − + e−+2H+→H2O2

In the presence of BQ, ·O2 − will be consumed, indirectly reducing the consumption of e− and thus improving the reduction efficiency of Cr (VI). The reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) did not change significantly after adding t-BuOH, indicating that ·OH was not an important active substance for the photo-reduction of Cr (VI). It should be noted that the addition of K2S2O8 reduced the reduction efficiency but not significantly, illustrating e− is the foremost active species for photo-reduction of Cr (VI). And the low reduction degree is due to the fact that CDs in the composite material has both electron-receiving and electron-donating properties and has the ability of rapid electron transfer.

Fig. 13(b) shows the effects of various trapping agents on the photodegradation of MB. The degradation efficiency of MB was almost unchanged in the existence of BQ, indicating that ·O2 − had no major influence on the degradation of MB. While the degradation efficiency decreased significantly in the existence of EDTA-2Na, suggesting that h+ was the dominant active species in the degradation of MB. It is worth noting that the degradation efficiency was slightly reduced in the presence of t-BuOH, suggesting that ·OH played a certain role in the degradation process, but the amount was probably not significant.

Based on the above research and analysis, we deduce the preliminary mechanism of Cr(VI) photo-reduction and MB photodegradation on LP-2 composites(Fig. 14 ). NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) has two main excitation pathways under visible light irradiation: (1) Photo-carriers can be generated by excitation of Fe–O clusters; (2) photo-generated electrons can be transferred to Fe–O clusters by –NH2 excitation(i.e., LMCT mechanism). These two mechanisms cooperate with each other to promote photocatalytic degradation [59]. The photo-generated electrons generated by the excitation of g-C3N4 were transferred to the valence band position of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) through the “bridge” formed by CDs, and then recombined with the above-mentioned h+. The effective electron transfer from g-C3N4 to NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) and the “neutralization” of e−/h+ pairs can effectively increase the electron hole separation rate of g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe), and improve the photocatalytic efficiency of the composites.

In summary, we propose a possible mechanism of photo-reduction of Cr (VI) and photodegradation of MB by a Z-scheme heterojunction composite photocatalyst: under visible light irradiation,e− of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) and g-C3N4 will be excited from VB to CB, and holes are left in VB. CDs forms a “bridge” structure on the interface of parent material under solvothermal conditions, photo-generated electrons in the g-C3N4 CB can be transferred to the VB of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) through the “bridge” of CDs and recombine with the holes on it. Therefore, holes are enriched in the VB of g-C3N4 respectively, which are specially used for degrading MB. While highly reducing photoelectric electrons (e−) are enriched in the conduction band of NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe), which are used for reducing heavy metal Cr (Ⅵ) ions. In the whole system, CDs not only acts as a “bridge” for electron transfer, but also acts as an electron donor and acceptor due to its excellent charge characteristics, which can not only provide e− for the reduction of Cr (Ⅵ), but also consume h+, effectively inhibiting e−/h+ recombination, thus improving the photocatalytic activity. It should be noted that ECB(NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)) < ECB(g-C3N4) < E0(·OH/OH−) = 1.99 eV, therefore, the composite material could not produce ·OH independently [60]. However, there were a series of transfer and transformation processes in the photocatalytic reaction, and a small amount of ·OH could be produced for the degradation of MB, but the main active species was h+ in the photodegradation process, which was consistent with the results of the capture experiment. Under light radiation, the whole photocatalytic system has the following transfer and transformation processes:

In summary, g-C3N4 nanosheets with good dispersion were prepared by nitric acid pretreatment and secondary thermal oxidation. Then, under solvothermal action, NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) and g-C3N4 formed a Z-scheme heterojunction. Glucose was used as the precursor to introduce CDs as the “bridge” of the heterojunction interface, and finally g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs was formed. The synthesized g-C3N4/NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe)/CDs composite photocatalyst demonstrated better photocatalytic activity in both Cr (Ⅵ) photo-reduction and MB photodegradation than that of the single component. The reduction rate of Cr (Ⅵ) by LP-x composite material can reach 90% after 90 min of visible light irradiation. And the degradation rate of MB by all composites was close to 100% after 120 min of light. It is worth noting that when the g-C3N4 dosage is 10 wt% (i.e., LP-2), the composite photocatalyst obtained has the best photo-reduction efficiency of Cr (Ⅵ) (45 min, about 99%) and photodegradation efficiency of MB (60 min, about 100%). The characterization results show that g-C3N4 and NH2-MIL-88 B(Fe) combine successfully and form a heterostructure, and CDs forms a “bridge” at the interface between the two. At the same time, the existence of CDs “bridge” can act as a local electron acceptor. It can also be used as electron donor to improve the photo-reduction efficiency of Cr (Ⅵ). The formation of heterostructures is beneficial to promote charge transfer and effectively promote the separation of photo-carriers, thus the photocatalytic activity was improved. Importantly, the results of four cycles experiments show that LP-2 have the characteristics of stability and efficiency. At present, many photocatalytic materials have unsatisfactory effects under extreme conditions, and often lose the stability of the materials while ensuring efficiency. Therefore, they are not suitable for large-scale industrial wastewater treatment. LP-2 has a good effect on the reduction of Cr (Ⅵ) under extreme acid conditions, indicating that it has great potential in the treatment of heavy metal ions in industrial wastewater under extreme acid conditions. More green synthesis methods and degradation of various pollutants under extreme conditions can be further explored in the subsequent research.
